Episode 572: Focused Invite Days

August 22, 2018 by Ivan Misner 8 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

Charlie Teixera from BNI Hawaii joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about focused invite days. By using this approach, Charlie’s chapter had a 25% increase in membership and a 35% increase in Thank You for Closed Business.

A focused invite day is a day each month where the whole chapter invites visitors who are in the featured presenter’s contact sphere. That means every member gets a day where the entire chapter is focused on bringing the most likely referral partners for that member to the meeting.

Leading up to these focused invite days, the leadership team provides education to the chapter members about what contact spheres are and ways in which members should invite visitors.

There are five key ingredients to the success of focused invite days:

  1. Support from area and regional directors and your chapter’s leadership team with the education process and the invitations.
  2. Asking all members to write down and share their reasons why growth is important and inviting visitors is advantageous.
  3. Consistent and kind reminders to the members in advance of the focused invite days, including recognition for those who have invited visitors already.
  4. Holding members accountable for following through with their invitations. It inspires everyone to see who has accepted invitations. Seeing your work pay off and how it helps your fellow members is a great motivator.
  5. Focused invite days help with retention by re-focusing on contact spheres. The chapter helps existing members recognize who can help them grow their business and then invites those people as visitors.

It takes a little time to build up momentum with focused invite days, but if you follow the process consistently every month, it will pay off.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 572 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at Global Support in Charolette, North Carolina this week, Priscilla. It’s always great to visit headquarters. I have a special guest today on BNI Podcast. Her name is Charlie Teixeira. She is a BNI member in Hawaii and she is the owner of North Shore Embroidery which runs the Route 99 Hawaii line. She’s been a member of BNI for 7 years and a Director Consultant for 4 years. Charlie, welcome to BNI Podcast!

Charlie:
Hello, Ivan. Good to be here!

Ivan:

It’s great to have you on. We are going to talk today about a concept that you have been applying to you chapter called “Focused Invite Days”. Your director [Joan Shiree] loved it so much. She said that I had to bring you on to the podcast, and you and I had met so I was happy to do that. In the last year- in the last roughly around June to May 2017 to 2018, you had a 25% net growth in your membership by using this approach- which is awesome! And which is incredibly impressive to me, is that you had a 35% increase in your Thank you for closed business. So, well done! And well done to you chapter! You went from the grey to the green in 1 year by using the things that you’re talking about with the “Focused Invite Days.”

So, your 5 key takeaways, let’s see if we can hit on as many of these as we can. First is “Support from your leadership.” Do you want to talk about that a little bit, Charlie?

Charlie:
Sure! Thanks for having me on, I really appreciate it! So, I kind of boiled down what I thought were the 5 key takeaways for having really successful “Focused Invite Days.” The first one is, having strong support from your leadership. And this comes from the Executive Director, as you mentioned [ Joanne Shuree] who held these numerous training webinars for all of our chapters in Hawaii, and they were recorded for incoming growth coordinators. So for anyone who in on the podcast and doesn’t know about “Focused Invite Days” what they are is you set up a time of the month- so for us at the North Shore chapter, its the third Thursday of each month where members are inviting specific people to listen to this Feature Presenter who are in the speakers “Contact Sphere”- we’ll come back to this. And leading up to these “Focused Invite Days” your providing education to the chapter members on what “Contact Spheres” are, the ways in which members should invite visitors – which includes using BNI Connect Global and just general stuff on how to invite.

So, we had Joanne running these webinars, and then we also had really good support from our leadership team in the chapter such as our President { names stated but didn’t look up } and our Director in Market theory all kind of walked the the walk and they were okay with holding members accountable and checking with members and saying or asking them, “How can we help you invite visitors?” So, that was key to having that support.

Ivan:
And that is helpful, but if you’re in a region where the Director is not doing that, how do you suggest they address that? Do maybe after chapter meetings discussions about this- just in case you don’t have a Director who is doing that.

Charlie:
I think that is a great question! Maybe just have an open discussion with the Director and say, “This is something that has worked for another region.”

Ivan:
Good point! So come listen to this podcast and do it in your region. Then of course you need to have the Leadership Team actively on board.

So, next thing I think you have in the key takeaways is, do chapter members really need to start this discussion with their “why”? You thought this was very important. Talk about that.

Charlie:
Yeah, I think this is actually one of the most important things is having member really consider for themselves why growth is important. Or why inviting visitors would be advantageous. So, I had members write this on a piece of paper and handed it in, I compiled it and then emailed it back to the group. I was actually really surprised by the variety and in-depth answers. As a former hippy teacher, one of the pieces of advice I got was, “To find a great discussion is asking questions to which you do not know all the answers.” I got other interesting surprising answers there.

Ivan:
Excellent! What else? Because I agree with you about asking your “why’s” being it is one fo the most important thing.

Charlie:
It builds a foundation that they can come back to later. So, the third key which I wanted to hone in on just a little bit is consistent “Be Kind” reminders to the members. So leading up to our “Focused Invite Day”, we were asking members to bring to the meeting 4-6 names of people they will be writing. Our meeting is on a Thursdays, typically right after the meeting I’ll send an email out to all of our members recognizing members who have invited visitors- which recognition being 1 of the “Be Nice” core values. And then the following Monday I’ll send out another email reminder letting them know who they should be inviting, and I think that tone is really important because as everyone knows, we are really busy and sometimes the reminders I would send out would be more gentle or funny. Just to kind of nudge members along to invite them. I think between being gentle and being bossy, I would be on the side of gentleness. Because hopefully the motivation for them inviting people would intrinsically come back to the “why’s” which they’ve already thought about.

Ivan, one of the biggest “why’s” for me, which happened maybe 4 months into this “Focused Invite Day” series, is that we had invited an interior decorator from one of our members to hear her speak, and we had 7 visitors that day. This member shared with her husband, who then shared with [ Joanne Shuree], that it really meant a lot to her and was really encouraging that everyone took the time to invite these people. And that just kind of hit home. This is a feeling you that you can’t fake of “Givers Gain.” I don’t know, I was kind of surprised at how emotionally engaged- even more engaged I became at that point. So, I just wanted to share that one story.

Ivan:
And that where- what you said to me that, “Tone is important.” Doing this as a way of helping one another and supporting one another to have a better team is all about that tone.

Charlie:
Correct. Definitely.

On the flip side of being kind and gentle is it also enforces accountability. We need to do more than just ask our members to invite visitors. IT’s asking them in the beginning, “Is it okay if you give me permission to hold you accountable?” So, it suggested in the BNI Coordinator manual to use a whiteboard or flip chart, and what we do is we have a white board and two weeks before “Focused Invite Day” we are saying to our members, “You will be asked to write 6 names of the people you are inviting.” Write them literally on the whiteboard, and the next week circle the names that RSVP-ed. You are actually aiming for 100% participation, so like a day before I’ll send out a text saying, “I’m filling out the board right now, just a (gentle) reminder to do that-“ You don’t want to embarrass anyone or call anyone out. But it really does work where everyone sees it on the board. It’s really exciting to have everyone in a chapter to write down all these names on the board.

Ivan:
And that process not only helps maintain accountability, but it also helps get engagement. That engagement is, what I believe leads to more effective accountability.

Charlie:
Yes, exactly! One of the long term lessons that I’ve learned was that it had this snowball/lava flowing technique where it a gated people more and –

Ivan:
You’re from Hawaii and so that “lava flowing” thing is really important!

Charlie:
It is! It probably is right now.

Ivan:
I’m sorry, I cut you off. Go ahead and finish your statement.

Charlie:
It’s just that flow where they are seeing this pay off and they are seeing how it’s helping their fellow members. It really does create this snowball/lava-ball effect.

Ivan:
Right. So, the last key takeaway is “Retention.” Talk a little bit about that and how that has anything to do with “Focused Invite Days.”

Charlie:
Well, this really ties in with a great presentation at the BNI National Convention. The talk- that I’m sure you remember was, “The most important thing is not growth; it’s retention.” And really refocusing back on context here. So you can grow your chapter by 10 members, but if you lose 20, you obviously have a net loss. So, it’s a really good education topic to talk about Contact Spheres. Specifically- this came from a chapter that I oversee that had a “Focused Invite Day” that had a credit card processing person in the chapter and we asked him, “Who do you want the members in the chapter to invite?” His immediate response was, “Anyone who uses credit card processing.” Of course that is not someone in his Contact Sphere. So, we are talking to him and saying, “Well maybe you can invite people from an alarm company, who already go into restaurants who have a similar target market.” Those are the people in that person’s Contact Sphere to invite. So, very similarly, this happened in out North Shore chapter where we had an [inaudible] representative and her immediate response was, “I want to have people come to my presentation who just want to change their health.” Which of course is broad. We had her narrow down and say we wanted to invite nutritionists because they share the same target market. Nutritionists would be talking to clients about their health. It was really helping build these Contact Spheres for members and as we’ve read that 60-70% of your business in BNI comes from your Contact Sphere.Which we see retention leads to sustained growth.

Ivan:
I couldn’t agree more!

Let me just clarify for those of you who are listening to the podcast what the difference between a Contact Sphere and a Power Team is. Contact Sphere is a group of business professionals that. Have a symbiotic relationship. A “Power Team” is the professional that you have a relationship with. So, the Contact Sphere are the people that could potentially fit within that symbiotic relationship. A Power Team are the people that you have a relationship with that fit within the Contact Sphere. So ithe idea is to look within your Contact Sphere to bring them to BNI so that they become a Power Team for you because they are a symbiotic profession. If you are focusing your inviting based on people who are in your Contact Sphere, you are going to increase your referrals at a great percentage. Hence that example of 25% net growth in membership, but a 35% increase in thank you for closed business.Does that sound right to you, Charlie?

Charlie:
It does.

Ivan:
Well, thank you so much for being on the podcast! Any last comments of thoughts you have about your “Focused Invite Days’?

Charlie:
Just that it takes a little bit of time and momentum to build. And just like BNI, it goes back to structure and doing it on a monthly basis.

Ivan:
Yes, I agree with that last thing a lot. It’s about having a plan and working the plan. So, if you talk this concept that Charlie is talking about here and you do it consistently, and get engagement from your local Executive Director or Director Consultant to help you, you too can have a substantial growth in you membership and your referrals.

Charlie, thanks so much for being on the podcast today. I really appreciate you and I appreciate you taking the time to be on the show!

Charlie:
I appreciate you so much, too. Thanks, Ivan and Priscilla. Aloha!

Ivan:
Aloha!

Over to you, Priscilla!

Priscilla:
Okay, great! Aloha to you both. Thank you so much! That was a wonderful podcast.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and I look forward to you joining us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Rohit Jain says

    August 23, 2018 at 5:59 am

    It would really be a great idea. The member making the presentation will have a wonderful opportunity to showcase and promote his business.

    Reply

  2. Phang Jai Juet says

    August 23, 2018 at 11:38 am

    Great podcast. My first attempt listening to it. Thank you.

    Reply

  3. Paula Barrios says

    August 23, 2018 at 2:30 pm

    Excelente idea!

    Reply

  4. Jaymi says

    August 27, 2018 at 12:17 pm

    When you are talking about inviting people in the contact sphere, is the emphasis on professions with vacancies in the chapter?

    Reply

  5. Mark Seery says

    August 31, 2018 at 2:42 pm

    Thank you Ivan, Charlie and Priscilla!

    Very informative! As you pointed out, results are so impressive. Following the plan, getting buy in from the chapter LT, members and of course Director Consultants and education (the Why), are the keys to accountability – ALL ingredients to success!

    More specifically from the synopsis;
    There are five key ingredients to the success of focused invite days:

    Support from area and regional directors and your chapter’s leadership team with the education process and the invitations.

    Asking all members to write down and share their reasons why growth is important and inviting visitors is advantageous.

    Consistent and kind reminders to the members in advance of the focused invite days, including recognition for those who have invited visitors already.

    Holding members accountable for following through with their invitations. It inspires everyone to see who has accepted invitations. Seeing your work pay off and how it helps your fellow members is a great motivator.

    Focused invite days help with retention by re-focusing on contact spheres. The chapter helps existing members recognize who can help them grow their business and then invites those people as visitors.

    Great Podcast and Mahalo!!!

    Reply

  6. Zitzi Martínez says

    September 5, 2018 at 1:16 am

    Extraordinaria estrategia para crecer de manera mucho más enfocada.

    Reply

  7. Sally Onesty says

    February 2, 2019 at 8:08 pm

    I love the idea of asking is it ok if we hold you accountable? Asking permission first in this area is something I never thought about. I’m going to try this.

    Reply

  8. Aarthy Rajagopalan says

    February 13, 2026 at 7:07 pm

    Intentional invites build intentional growth, structure and consistency make the difference.

    Reply
